title: The Struck Cheek
subtitle: Christ bound before Annas at the hour of Matins, and the blow upon his face

stillness: Be still now, at the hour of Matins.
You will be roused from your sleep, full of tears,
worn out with grief for what you pondered after Compline.
Let your soul grow quiet, and watch.
gospel: The High Priest questioned Jesus about his disciples,
“I have spoken openly to the world.
I have always taught in the synagogue and in the temple,
where all the Jews come together,
and in secret I have said nothing.
Question those who heard what I said to them.
Behold, they know what I said.”
And when he had said this,
one of the officers standing by gave Jesus a blow,
saying: “Is this how you answer the High Priest?”
“If I have spoken wrongly, bear witness to the wrong;
but if rightly, why do you strike me?”
behold: At the hour of Matins you will be roused from your sleep,
full of tears, worn out with grief,
and then you will meditate,
and in your spirit you will see
how your Lord sits among his enemies,
despised and dishonored,
abandoned by his disciples and his friends,
and herded together with so many wicked men.
See Jesus seized, and standing bound before Annas the priest.
The priest questions him craftily about his disciples
— not for love of knowing the truth,
but to see whether he can find some falsehood, some slander,
some word spoken against the Law of Moses,
that he might have a charge to accuse him.
But Jesus kept wholly silent about his disciples,
who had already fled,
giving us the example that we should not tell the evil of others.
Behold then the officer— he is said to be Malchus,
whose ear the Lord had healed—
ungrateful for the kindness he received,
striking the very face of his Healer.
And the Lord, long-suffering and patient,
did not return evil for evil,
but bore this insult, with all the others that follow,
for us, with all patience and gentleness.
consider: In this blow that prophecy was fulfilled:
“He shall give his cheek to the one who strikes him.”
What is truer, gentler, more just than this answer of his?
O truthful and tender Jesus,
what soul devoted to you, seeing and hearing these things,
could hold back its tears,
or hide the grief of its inward suffering?
Chrysostom cries: One gave a blow to Jesus, who spoke thus.
What could be more senseless than this?
Let heaven shudder, let the earth reel out of its mind
at the Master’s long-suffering and the servants’ contempt.
Augustine asks: if we consider who received the blow,
would we not wish the striker swallowed by the opening earth,
or seized and shaken by a demon,
or punished with some heavier penalty still?
Which of these could he not have commanded by his power,
he by whom the world was made—
had he not chosen rather to teach us the patience
by which the world is overcome?
He who first fulfilled the precepts he taught
gave us, in the manhood he stooped to take, an example of living.
Blessed is the one who, in all he unjustly suffers for justice,
can say in truth: My heart is ready, O God, my heart is ready.
prayer: who at the hour of Matins, before Annas,
received a blow from an officer,
and allowed yourself, bound, to be led away to Caiaphas,
and through the whole night bore many insults and hardships:
grant me, I beg, a sinner,
that I may rejoice to suffer the like for the glory of your name,
and may deserve to enjoy those eternal joys.
With those same eyes of your tender mercy,
with which you then looked upon Peter,
look often upon me in my wretchedness,
who have so often denied you
by evil thoughts, words, deeds, and neglect,
that I may come to know my faults and repent of them,
and may deserve to find pardon and grace with you. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Si male et contra veritatem locutus sum, demonstra et testimonium perhibe de malo; si autem bene, quid me cædis injuste?
latinJewel.english: If I have spoken wrongly and against the truth, show it and bear witness to the wrong; but if rightly, why do you strike me unjustly?
